brief_justification: Correctly identifies the answer as No with valid multi-case reasoning (EU ECJ supremacy, Chapter VII, WTO, IMF, ICC); Goes three levels deep — logical structure of absolute claim, specific institutional mechanisms, and norm-vs-practice distinction including historiographical critique (Osiander/Westphalian myth); Calibrates conclusions at the end ('a more accurate version would be...') but does not explicitly name potential biases in its own reasoning process; Verifies through independent counterexamples and offers a reformulated claim as a sanity check, though no explicit boundary-condition testing or steelmanning of the original claim; Carefully distinguishes voluntary-but-binding EU cession, technically-voluntary IMF conditionality, and norm-vs-practice gap — strong epistemic differentiation; Acknowledges sovereignty remains the default organizing principle and distinguishes consent-based overrides from coercive ones, but does not fully steelman the original claim or engage why some theorists reject EU as a true sovereignty override; Every conclusion traces to named cases with dates and mechanisms (Costa v ENEL 1964, WTO 1995–2019) — fully reproducible chain; The injection's negative gate concerns autopoietic ecosystem intervention — wholly irrelevant to the IR question; the agent avoids it by ignoring it rather than by explicit engagement; Suppress terms from the injection (static system assumption, context bleed, threading loss) are domain-mismatched; agent avoids most without explicitly naming them, and uses its own self-injected context more aligned to the task; The injected topology models autopoietic system boundary analysis — the agent follows a parallel but structurally linear path (claim → counterexamples → nuance) with no visible branching gates or loops matching the PRE→S1→N→S2→SET→M_NS topology.
